text: The surgeon uses an eighteen-inch Stratafix suture to pass through the anterior abdominal wall just below the costal margin, aiming towards the right crus. The tip-up and arm four are open to assist in lifting the liver. A vessel sealer is used to go through the right crus and return to the anterior abdominal wall, effectively retracting the liver to achieve sufficient exposure for the operation.

A sponge is placed on the right side of the screen for easy access. The surgeon uses a tip-up instrument on the gastrocolic ligament to lift the stomach, creating tension for dissection. The distal dissection begins, progressing up the stomach with a 4D visage aiding in stomach retraction. The surgeon advances along the gastro-splenic ligament, maintaining the position of arm four until necessary.

The surgeon adjusts the positioning of the sponge to retract tissues to the right side, exposing the left crus. The anesthesiologist retracts the Visi-G, while the surgeon uses arms three and four to pull the stomach and align the stapler. A bougie is passed down to ensure no obstruction, followed by the application of the blue load for the first staple fire.

The surgeon positions the tip-up instrument on arm four to lift the stomach and ensure no posterior structures are caught. The stapler is fired along the stomach, with the surgeon using a bipolar device to control mild bleeding at the distal staple line. Arm four is repositioned higher up on the stomach as the stapling progresses.

The surgeon uses arm three to position the stomach by sliding the posterior fundus laterally while the bedside assistant prepares the stapler. Arm four continues to straighten the stomach as the vessel sealer sweeps out, addressing minor bleeding on the specimen side. A white load stapler is introduced, and the surgeon carefully avoids stapling the fat pad during dissection up to the left crus.

The surgeon removes the sponge from the surgical field to prevent it from being stapled or obstructing the view. A final staple is fired laterally to the fat pad. As the stapler fires, the vessel sealer and tip-up instrument are withdrawn. A SertaCell is applied to the staple line, and the vessel sealer is reintroduced through arm four. A leak test is conducted on the staple line.
The surgeon places SertaCell on the staple line, ensuring the vessel sealer in arm four is ready to divide the stitch. The specimen and sponge are positioned for easy extraction.
